nodes.moveReason: Jump the knight to the active c6 outpost with check, forcing Black to stay on the back rank while White starts building the handoff cage.
Use the forcing knight check to enter the longer preparation route; from d5 the knight can pivot through f4 and g6 while the king holds the center.
Centralize the knight to d4 so it can reach c6 or the f5/e2 transfer squares without dropping the king or bishop below the fourth rank.
Step the king to f6 so the knight on c6 and bishop on e6 can keep Black boxed while White approaches the final side-adjacent shape.
Bring the king closer to the trapped king; with Black already on f8, White needs king pressure before the knight can finish the handoff.
Move into opposition on d6, taking the key square beside the knight so Black cannot use the d-file to loosen the cage.
Take d6 to keep the knight protected and to prepare a direct finish if Black stays on the back rank.
Retreat the knight to f4 to preserve the bishop color parity and start the g6 triangulation route against Black's f8 defense.
Bring the king to d6, using the knight on d4 as a shield while White takes the opposition squares around d8.
Send the knight to c6 to check the king's escape squares and reconnect with the standard prepared pattern.
Use the king move to f6 to cut off e7 and g7, making the knight transfer to c6 or f5 safe.
Move the knight to e5 so it can jump to f7 and complete the same-color prepared shape next move.
The black king is restricted, so White can freely triangulate with the king to achieve the preferred parity before the knight finishes.
Shift the knight to f4 to keep the d6 king active while setting up the g6 and e2 transfer motifs.
Use the knight retreat to f4 to keep the position won against the f8 defense and preserve the bishop's color anchor.
Jump to b6 to cover d7 and force Black into the final edge pattern; the king on d6 already controls the cage.
Improve the king to f5 before moving the knight; White must keep Black boxed while preparing the g6 check.
Check from g6 to drive Black back to the edge and place the knight on the same color complex as the bishop.
Move the knight to g6 without check because Black is already contained; this sets up king triangulation around f5 and e5.
Transfer the knight through e2 so it can return to f4 with the right color and without blocking the king on d6.
Put the knight back on c6, restoring the familiar cage with the king on f6 ready to finish.
This is the direct handoff: the knight reaches f7, side-adjacent to both kings and matching the bishop's square color.
Continue the triangulation from n23; the king steps to g6 to keep Black restricted while preserving the move parity White needs.
Move the king to d7 to take the last flight squares and force Black into a back-rank response.
Send the knight to g6 so it can return through e5 or f4 while the king on d6 keeps Black boxed.
Use the king to approach from e7, keeping Black away from the bishop and setting up the final knight placement.
Move the knight to g6 because the king already owns d6; the knight now has the right color route back to the edge cage.
Finish immediately with the knight on d7, adjacent to both kings and on the bishop's color.
Check from g6 to gain time and force Black toward the corner before White walks the king into the final net.
Move the king to f6 to keep the h-file king trapped and prepare the knight's final return to g6.
Step to g5 to shoulder the king on h7 and keep the knight's route to g6 available.
Return the king to f6, keeping Black on h6 boxed while preserving the same-color knight finish.
Bring the king to d6 so the advanced knight on g6 has support and Black cannot escape through e7.
Take d6 to hold the center; with the knight already on g6, White only needs to convert the edge cage.
Bring the knight back to f4, restoring the working color and setting up the final g6 or e2 route.
Use f4 as the knight's transfer square; it keeps Black boxed while avoiding a premature wrong-color finish.
Move the king back to f6 to maintain opposition; the knight on c6 is already close to the final pattern.
Step to e7 to cut off Black's king from the bishop and force it toward the prepared back-rank cage.
Triangulate with the king to d5, changing the move order while the knight on g6 keeps Black restricted.
Move to f6, taking the h-file king's escape squares and preparing the knight to return with correct parity.
Use Kf7 to keep contact with the h7 king and prevent Black from slipping out of the edge net.
Return to f6 so the king holds h6 in the box while the knight remains ready to finish.
Bring the knight to e5, the final transfer square before Nd7 completes the prepared shape.
Move to g5 to keep Black's king confined on g7 while preserving the knight's same-color landing square.
Continue the triangulation by stepping to e5; White is adjusting king parity while Black remains restricted.
Finish with Ng6: the knight is adjacent to both kings and matches the bishop's square color.
Return the king to f6 to keep the h8 king boxed and make the knight's g6 finish decisive.
Move the knight to g6, tightening the cage before the king finishes the opposition pattern.
Step to c6 to triangulate from the d-file and force Black back into the final Nd7 handoff.
Move to f6, keeping the h8 king on the edge and preserving the knight's direct route to g6.
Return to f6 to hold the h6 king in place and avoid giving up the edge cage.
Finish with Nd7: the knight becomes side-adjacent to both kings on the bishop's color.
Move the king to f6 so the knight on g6 and bishop on e6 lock the final h-file cage.
Return to d6, keeping the king close enough to support the final Nd7 or Ng6 handoff.
